FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. ABCD Study; specifically states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study" and describes EARS passive sensing subsample from years 4, 5, and 6

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: EARS app passive sensing data on 640 participants; characterizes smartphone app usage during school hours with statistical methods and descriptive findings

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper characterizes app usage by category (social, communication, entertainment, games, etc.) but reports only descriptive statistics (mean app times) with no analysis pairing smartphone/social-media use as IV or DV with an individual-trait-level outcome variable. This is descriptive characterization, not association analysis.

notes: Paper does not report inferential associations between smartphone/SM use and individual-trait outcomes; FT-EC2 applies (no qualifying smartphone/SM variable pairing)

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes prospective data from the U.S. ABCD Study; Years 2 (2018–2020, ages 11–12) and Year 4 (2020–2022, ages 13–14); states "5356 US adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis: Poisson and linear regression models examining prospective associations between exposures (Year 2) and BMI outcomes (Year 4) with numeric quantitative results reported

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is measured as a composite: "asking adolescents to estimate the average number of hours they spend using screens recreationally (e.g., watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat)." The criteria state: "Composite 'screen time' totals that bundle TV/console/phone with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM" do NOT qualify. Methods describe TV, video games, texting, social media, and video chat combined without per-modality breakdown isolating phone or social media.

notes: Although the paper reports robust quantitative results (RR, β coefficients), screen time exposure fails FT-IC2 because it bundles multiple modalities without isolating phone/SM. FT-EC2 applies.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; specifically states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (year 3: 2019–2021 to year 5: 2021–2023)" and "7407 adolescents"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis: mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models examining prospective associations between parental media practices (Year 3) and video game behaviors (Years 4 and 5) with reported odds ratios and beta coefficients

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in World Journal of Pediatrics, 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: The paper measures parental media practices as independent variables and video game use as dependent variable. Video gaming alone is not a qualifying smartphone or social media variable under the criteria. The criteria specify "smartphone use" and "social media use" as qualifying exposures; video games are explicitly listed as excluded in §2.4: "TV-only · console/PC-gaming-only · unspecified-platform-gaming-only" are non-qualifying. No separately-measured smartphone or social media variable analyzed as IV or DV.

notes: Paper focuses on video game use, not smartphone or social media use. Video gaming alone does not meet FT-IC2 criterion. FT-EC2 applies.
